name: Arturo Sandoval
description: Cuban jazz trumpeter, pianist and composer
abstract: Arturo Sandoval (born November 6, 1949) is a Cuban-American jazz trumpeter, pianist, timbalero, and composer. While living in his native Cuba, Sandoval was influenced by jazz musicians Charlie Parker, Clifford Brown, and Dizzy Gillespie.

interviews.title: Arturo Sandoval interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 12, 2023

works.t: Every Day I Think of You

works_created.t: Every Day I Think of You

quotes.text: Yeah because jazz is not elevator music. It’s something you have to concentrate when you listen, and you can establish a kind of conversation thing pretending that you will understand what’s going on on stage or in a recording or whatever. You have to pay attention and you have to try to follow what they’re doing and what the artist are sharing with us. And if you don’t do that jazz is — one more time, it’s not elevator music. It’s not a background music. It’s not something that you put there when you’re having dinner and forget about it at a very low volume. No, no. That’s not jazz music. It’s like going to the concert hall and listening to Mozart or Chopin or Stravinsky. You can’t establish a conversation in Carnegie Hall, meanwhile you listen to a concerto. This is exactly the same thing. And also, talking about support, the few poor stations that play jazz, they are community radio, very small, with a very short antenna and, you know, the reach they got is very poor and local. As soon as you move a little bit away from the station you lost the signal. That’s another crime. Because the trash music got a huge antenna. And you can listen all over. And you can listen very easy everywhere. But it’s another thing you know. They don’t spend resources in promoting our beloved music style.
quotes.caption: General analogy comparing jazz to classical music at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Arturo Sandoval interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 12, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Paquito D'Rivera
about_quotes.evidence: Well, the thing was that Arturo Sandoval... he came down. He say Dizzy Gillespie... And then he took him to my house, and I was not there.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paquito D'Rivera — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Altman
about_quotes.evidence: we got Arturo Sandoval to play a solo and then we got the big brass section. But it’s still recognizably Hans Zimmer’s music you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Altman interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, September 14, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Curtis Fuller
about_quotes.evidence: I mean, John Faddis was a really high note player, or Arturo Sandoval, when you hear them play, they don’t have to wait to…

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Palmieri
about_quotes.evidence: Arturo Sandoval wins that year, who did an album called Danzon. He’d just come from Cuba; he had all the hype and all that.

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Scott
about_quotes.evidence: Arturo Sandoval, Ray Parker and Michael McDonald did a whole bunch of vocals on one of his tunes.

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Alkyer
about_quotes.evidence: I went to see Arturo live. And he goes, “Frank, what are you doing?”
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Alkyer interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Orleans, Louisiana, January 10, 2020
